title: Maple Leaf Rag
context: Published 1899 by John Stark & Son of Sedalia, Missouri, it became the first instrumental sheet music to sell over a million copies.

title: The Entertainer
context: Published by John Stark in 1902, it became famous decades later as the theme music for the 1973 Oscar-winning film The Sting.

title: Frog Legs Rag
context: James Scott's first Stark-published rag, released December 1906, launched his career as one of ragtime's Big Three composers.

title: Elite Syncopations
context: Written in 1902, three years after Joplin's Maple Leaf Rag, and published in 1903 by John Stark & Son.

title: Livery Stable Blues
searchQuery: Original Dixieland Jass Band Livery Stable Blues
context: Recorded Feb 26, 1917 by the Original Dixieland Jass Band, it became the first jazz record ever commercially released, as Victor 18255.

title: Dipper Mouth Blues
context: Recorded April 6, 1923 at Gennett, featuring King Oliver's famed muted-cornet solo with 21-year-old Louis Armstrong on second cornet.

title: Wild Cat Blues
context: Recorded July 30, 1923 by Clarence Williams' Blue Five, it is Sidney Bechet's recording debut and Fats Waller's first recorded composition.

title: Black Bottom Stomp
searchQuery: Jelly Roll Morton Black Bottom Stomp
context: Recorded September 15, 1926 in Chicago for Victor by Jelly Roll Morton's Red Hot Peppers, a session that helped define hot jazz.

title: Heebie Jeebies
context: Recorded February 26, 1926 for Okeh in Chicago, its scat-singing chorus became the recording that popularized the technique in jazz.

title: West End Blues
context: Recorded June 28, 1928, its unaccompanied 15-second trumpet introduction by Armstrong is among the most influential passages in jazz history.

title: Snake Rag
context: Composed and first recorded by King Oliver's Creole Jazz Band at Gennett on April 6, 1923, the same session date as Dippermouth Blues.

title: Singin' the Blues
context: Recorded Feb 4, 1927 with Frankie Trumbauer and Eddie Lang; inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 1977.

title: Nobody's Sweetheart
context: Recorded in Chicago on Dec 16, 1927 by McKenzie & Condon's Chicagoans, released in 1928 on OKeh.

title: The Eel
context: Recorded Oct 21, 1933 with Eddie Condon's band, introducing Freeman's original tune named for his snaking improvisations.

title: Relaxin' at the Touro
searchQuery: Muggsy Spanier Relaxin at the Touro
context: Named for the New Orleans hospital where Spanier recovered from a near-fatal illness in 1938; became his Ragtime Band's theme.

title: It Don't Mean a Thing (If It Ain't Got That Swing)
searchQuery: Duke Ellington It Don't Mean a Thing
context: Recorded Feb 2, 1932 for Brunswick, the first Ellington record to feature singer Ivie Anderson; inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2008.

title: One O'Clock Jump
context: Recorded July 7, 1937 for Decca; the band-riff blues became the Count Basie Orchestra's closing theme for the next five decades.

title: Song of India
context: Recorded Jan 29, 1937 for Victor with a trumpet solo by Bunny Berigan, coupled on the same disc as Dorsey's hit Marie.

title: Begin the Beguine
context: Recorded July 24, 1938 for RCA Bluebird; the swing arrangement became one of 1938's best-selling records and made Shaw a star.

title: Sing, Sing, Sing
context: Recorded July 6, 1937 in Hollywood; the two-sided 78 featured one of the first extended drum solos on record, by Gene Krupa.

title: In the Mood
context: Recorded Aug 1, 1939 for Bluebird; sales topped a million copies and the record entered the Grammy Hall of Fame in 1983.

title: Take the A Train
searchQuery: Duke Ellington Take the A Train
context: Composed by Billy Strayhorn and first commercially recorded Feb 15, 1941; became Ellington's signature tune after a 1940 ASCAP dispute.

title: For Dancers Only
context: Composed and arranged by trumpeter Sy Oliver, recorded June 15, 1937 for Decca; Oliver re-recorded the same arrangement with his own band in 1950.

title: Woodchopper's Ball
context: Herman's 1939 Decca recording became his biggest hit, eventually selling over a million copies and entering the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2002.

title: Flying Home
context: The May 26, 1942 Decca cut features 19-year-old Illinois Jacquet's tenor solo, widely credited as a founding moment of R&B saxophone style.

title: Ko-Ko
context: Recorded Nov 26, 1945 for Savoy as Parker's first date as leader; a contrafact on Cherokee's changes, with Dizzy Gillespie sitting in on piano.

title: Salt Peanuts
context: Co-written by Gillespie and drummer Kenny Clarke in 1941; the classic version was cut May 11, 1945 for Guild with Parker, Haig, Russell and Catlett.

title: Shaw 'Nuff
searchQuery: Charlie Parker Dizzy Gillespie Shaw Nuff
context: Recorded May 11, 1945 for Guild Records and named for Gillespie's booking agent Billy Shaw; built on the I Got Rhythm chord changes.

title: 'Round Midnight
context: First recorded by Monk as a leader on Nov 21, 1947 for Blue Note at WOR Studios, a restrained 3:11 single take on the tune.

title: Tempus Fugue-It
context: Recorded in 1949 with Ray Brown on bass and Max Roach on drums, later released on Clef/Verve as part of the Jazz Giant album.

title: Ornithology
context: Recorded March 28, 1946 for Dial at Radio Recorders in Hollywood; co-written with trumpeter Benny Harris as a contrafact on How High the Moon.

title: Confirmation
context: Parker composed it in 1945, but the first studio recording was by Gillespie's group without Parker for Dial in Feb 1946.

title: Straight, No Chaser
context: Recorded July 23, 1951 at WOR Studios for the Genius of Modern Music sessions, with Sahib Shihab, Milt Jackson, Al McKibbon and Art Blakey.

title: Boplicity
context: Miles Davis credited Boplicity's composition to "Cleo Henry," his mother's maiden name, when it was recorded for Birth of the Cool in 1949.

title: Early Autumn
context: Getz's eight-bar solo on Woody Herman's Second Herd recording of Early Autumn, as a Four Brothers sax section member, made him a star.

title: Line for Lyons
context: Recorded by Gerry Mulligan's pianoless quartet with Chet Baker at San Francisco's Black Hawk club in 1952, named for DJ Jimmy Lyons.

title: Django
context: John Lewis wrote Django in 1954 as a tribute to guitarist Django Reinhardt, who had died the year before; it became the MJQ's signature tune.

title: My Funny Valentine
context: From Chet Baker's debut vocal album Chet Baker Sings, recorded Feb 15, 1954 for Pacific Jazz; the album entered the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2001.

title: Take Five
context: Take Five was the first jazz single to sell a million copies; Brubeck's quartet nailed it in two takes on July 1, 1959 after an earlier session failed.

title: Blue in Green
context: Though Kind of Blue credits only Miles Davis, Bill Evans said he wrote Blue in Green's melody and changes, crediting it Davis-Evans on his own 1959 album.

title: Strange Fruit
context: Columbia refused to record the anti-lynching song, so Holiday cut it for Commodore Records on April 20, 1939; it became her best-selling record.

title: Unforgettable
context: Nat King Cole recorded Unforgettable on Aug 17, 1951 with a Nelson Riddle arrangement; the song's working title had been Uncomparable.

title: Lullaby of Birdland
context: Sarah Vaughan cut the first vocal version of Lullaby of Birdland in December 1954, backed by trumpeter Clifford Brown, for EmArcy Records.

title: Summertime
context: On this 1957 Porgy and Bess duet, Louis Armstrong plays a trumpet intro before Ella Fitzgerald begins singing, produced by Norman Granz.

title: Fever
context: Lee wrote the Romeo loved Juliet verses herself, uncredited; the track earned three nominations at the first-ever Grammy Awards.

title: Mack the Knife
context: Recorded live in Berlin in Feb 1960, Fitzgerald forgot the lyrics and improvised on the spot, winning a Grammy for the performance.

title: Fly Me to the Moon
searchQuery: Frank Sinatra Fly Me to the Moon
context: Quincy Jones's 4/4 big-band arrangement for Sinatra and Basie became the first music played on the Moon, by Buzz Aldrin in 1969.

title: What a Wonderful World
searchQuery: Louis Armstrong What a Wonderful World
context: Recorded overnight in Las Vegas in 1967, it topped the UK chart in 1968, making Armstrong the oldest man ever to reach UK number one.

title: The Look of Love
searchQuery: Diana Krall The Look of Love
context: Claus Ogerman arranged and conducted the London Symphony Orchestra on this track, recorded at Abbey Road Studios in 2001.

title: The Preacher
context: Blue Note's Alfred Lion nearly cut this 1955 track as too old-timey, but Art Blakey and Silver threatened to cancel the session over it.

title: Joy Spring
searchQuery: Clifford Brown Max Roach Joy Spring
context: Clifford Brown named this 1954 tune after his pet name for his wife Larue, whom Max Roach had introduced to him.

title: St. Thomas
context: Rollins adapted this calypso from a Caribbean folk melody tied to his family's Virgin Islands roots for 1956's Saxophone Colossus.

title: Moanin'
context: Composed by pianist Bobby Timmons, this track's popularity led fans to rename the whole 1958 album Moanin' after it.

title: This Here
context: Recorded live at San Francisco's Jazz Workshop, this Bobby Timmons tune was a surprise hit that helped launch the soul-jazz style.

title: The Sidewinder
context: Recorded for Blue Note in December 1963, this became Lee Morgan's biggest hit; the first 4,000-copy pressing sold out within days.

title: Song for My Father
searchQuery: Horace Silver Song for My Father
context: The album cover features a photo of Silver's father, John Tavares Silver, a Cape Verdean immigrant to whom the tune is dedicated.

title: Speak No Evil
context: Recorded Christmas Eve 1964 at Van Gelder Studio, one of several Blue Note dates Shorter cut during his first year in Miles Davis's quintet.

title: So What
context: Recorded March 2, 1959, at Columbia's 30th Street Studio essentially as first complete takes, from only Davis's sketched modal ideas.

title: All Blues
context: A 6/8 twelve-bar blues recorded April 22, 1959, for Kind of Blue, built from only Davis's sketched melodic ideas with no rehearsal.

title: My Favorite Things
context: Marked Coltrane's recorded debut on soprano saxophone, an instrument Miles Davis had bought him during a 1960 European tour.

title: Impressions
context: The title track was recorded live at the Village Vanguard in November 1961, the same engagement that produced Live at the Village Vanguard.

title: Waltz for Debby
context: Captured live at the Village Vanguard on June 25, 1961, ten days before bassist Scott LaFaro died in a car accident.

title: Maiden Voyage
context: Recorded in a single day, March 17, 1965, at Van Gelder Studio with Hancock's Miles Davis Quintet bandmates Ron Carter and Tony Williams.

title: Free Jazz
context: Recorded in one continuous 36-minute take by a double quartet, with no overdubs or edits, at A&R Studios on December 21, 1960.

title: Ghosts
context: Cut July 10, 1964, for Bernard Stollman's ESP-Disk label, the first jazz recording that fledgling label ever released.

title: Ascension
context: Recorded June 28, 1965, with an expanded 11-piece ensemble including Pharoah Sanders and Archie Shepp, marking Coltrane's turn toward free jazz.

title: Conquistador
context: Recorded Oct 6, 1966 at Van Gelder Studio; the second of two Blue Note albums Cecil Taylor released that year, after Unit Structures.

title: Space Is the Place
searchQuery: Sun Ra Space Is the Place
context: The title track was recorded in one day, Oct 19 1972, in Chicago with Sun Ra's 19-piece Intergalactic Infinity Arkestra.

title: Attica Blues
context: Recorded Jan 24-26 1972 for Impulse!, just months after the Attica prison uprising was crushed, killing 43 inmates and hostages.

title: Bitches Brew
context: The title track was the first piece recorded, on Aug 19 1969, cut live with no overdubs during Columbia's 3-day Bitches Brew sessions.

title: Chameleon
context: Chameleon's famous bassline was played by Hancock on an ARP Odyssey synthesizer, from Head Hunters, the era's best-selling jazz LP.

title: Spain
searchQuery: Chick Corea Return to Forever Spain
context: Spain's intro quotes Joaquin Rodrigo's Concierto de Aranjuez; recorded Oct 1972 at IBC Studios, London, for Light as a Feather.

title: Birds of Fire
context: The title track's main riff runs in 18/8 (two bars of 9/8), from Mahavishnu Orchestra's last studio LP with its original lineup.

title: Red Clay
context: Recorded Jan 27-29 1970 at Van Gelder Studio, Red Clay was Hubbard's first album for Creed Taylor's new CTI label.

title: Birdland
context: Written by Joe Zawinul, Birdland earned Jaco Pastorius a Grammy nod for Best Jazz Soloist; Heavy Weather entered the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2011.

title: Come On, Come Over
searchQuery: Jaco Pastorius Come On Come Over
context: Come On, Come Over features Sam and Dave on vocals, from Jaco Pastorius's self-titled 1976 debut album produced by Bobby Colomby.

title: Street Life
context: Street Life hit number 5 on the UK singles chart with Randy Crawford vocals; Joe Sample based it on ski-slope chaos at Mammoth Mountain.

title: Are You Going with Me?
searchQuery: Pat Metheny Group Are You Going with Me
context: Are You Going with Me? earned a 1983 Grammy nomination for Best Instrumental Composition.

title: Black Codes (From the Underground)
searchQuery: Wynton Marsalis Black Codes From the Underground
context: Title track of the album that won two Grammy Awards in 1986, Best Jazz Instrumental Performance Soloist and Group.

title: Sing a Song of Song
searchQuery: Kenny Garrett Sing a Song of Song
context: First studio recording of this now-standard concert closer, from Garrett's Songbook, his first LP of all-original compositions.

title: Exit Music (For a Film)
searchQuery: Brad Mehldau Exit Music For a Film

title: Afro Blue
context: Opens Black Radio, featuring Erykah Badu on vocals; the album won the Grammy for Best R&B Album in 2013.

title: Black Gold
context: First single from Radio Music Society, released February 1, 2012, the first day of Black History Month.

title: Change of the Guard
searchQuery: Kamasi Washington Change of the Guard
context: Opens Kamasi Washington's nearly three-hour album The Epic, his 2015 debut for Brainfeeder, running over 12 minutes.

title: Hypopono
context: From GoGo Penguin's v2.0, which was shortlisted for the 2014 Mercury Prize.

title: Lingus
context: Recorded live before a studio audience in Utrecht for We Like It Here, built around Cory Henry's extended keyboard solo.
